pathway_description: Meteorological or agricultural drought reducing water availability during the cropping season.
stress_description: Farmers report insufficient water for crop production, particularly for rabi and zaid seasons or during dry spells in kharif.

context.aer_reference: AER-10: Central Highlands (Malwa, Bundelkhand and Eastern Satpura), hot sub-humid ecoregion | states=['Madhya Pradesh', 'Uttar Pradesh (Bundelkhand)', 'Rajasthan (parts)'] | rainfall=900-1200mm | aquifer=sedimentary_soft_rock

condition.qualitative_description: Three or more severe drought weeks during the kharif season, or a combination of at least one severe week and four or more moderate drought weeks, indicating persistent crop water stress.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Persistent severe drought weeks during kharif directly cause crop failure by exceeding the buffering capacity of the root zone and soil moisture reserves. In the sub-humid Bundelkhand and Malwa plateaus, soils are often shallow over semi-consolidated rock, limiting plant-available water capacity. Even moderate drought weeks compound stress when they occur at critical growth stages such as flowering or grain fill. SPI-based drought classification aligned with the India Drought Manual identifies these thresholds as operationally significant for kharif rice and soybean yield loss.

condition.qualitative_description: 3-month SPI at or below -1.0 (moderate meteorological drought) coinciding with VCI below 40 (moderate to severe vegetation stress), confirming both meteorological and agricultural drought are active simultaneously.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The India Drought Manual recommends combining SPI (meteorological drought) with VCI (agricultural drought via NDVI-based vegetation condition) to classify drought type and severity. Studies in Uttar Pradesh districts including Allahabad and Kanpur show that SPI and VCI during July–October are significantly correlated with kharif rice productivity index (r=0.61–0.70), explaining 69–76% of yield variability. When both indices simultaneously indicate moderate-to-severe drought, the pathway is confirmed as meteorological drought translating into agricultural water stress.

condition.qualitative_description: Four or more weeks under defined dry spell conditions (4-week moving window with less than 50% of historical rainfall) during the kharif season, disrupting critical crop growth stages.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Prolonged dry spells within an otherwise sub-humid season are a characteristic drought pattern in Bundelkhand and Malwa, where monsoon rainfall is spatially and temporally erratic despite adequate annual totals. Dry spells of four or more weeks disrupt crop water demand-supply synchronisation, particularly during vegetative and reproductive stages. Management of crop water under drought emphasises that intermittent droughts can be buffered via the root system, but shallow soils over semi-consolidated aquifer terrain limit this buffering, making dry spell duration a critical diagnostic variable.

condition.qualitative_description: Seasonal (kharif) rainfall below 75% of the long-term seasonal mean, or annual rainfall below 80% of the long-term annual mean, confirming a below-normal precipitation year consistent with meteorological drought.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In AER-10, annual rainfall of 900–1200mm supports rainfed kharif crops, but inter-annual variability is high. A seasonal deficit below 75% of the mean is sufficient to trigger agricultural drought even in sub-humid zones. Annual precipitation deficits also reduce groundwater recharge in semi-consolidated aquifers, compounding water unavailability for rabi irrigation. GRACE-based studies confirm that after drought years, groundwater storage drops and fails to recover fully even in subsequent good monsoon years, amplifying multi-season water scarcity.

condition.qualitative_description: Monsoon onset delayed by more than 14 days beyond the climatological normal onset date for the district, shortening the effective kharif growing season.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Delayed monsoon onset in Bundelkhand and Malwa shortens the effective kharif season, compressing the window for sowing, vegetative growth, and grain fill. A delay of more than two weeks forces farmers to either sow into dry soil (risking germination failure) or delay sowing (risking terminal drought at grain fill). This signal amplifies the severity of any concurrent dry spell or SPI deficit but does not independently confirm the drought pathway.

overall_reasoning_note: Drought as a causal pathway for water scarcity in AER-10 sub-humid zones should be confirmed by at least two primary signals: SPI/VCI co-occurrence (sig_02) and either severe drought weeks (sig_01) or seasonal precipitation deficit (sig_04). Dry spell duration (sig_03) and delayed onset (sig_05) are amplifying signals that increase severity but should not be used alone to confirm the pathway. The key distinction from groundwater stress as the primary pathway is that drought signals precede or co-occur with any well-level decline, and SOGE values in the block remain within safe limits. Always verify whether the water scarcity is confined to kharif dry spells (drought pathway) or persists into rabi despite normal monsoon (groundwater depletion pathway). Recommended interventions — check dam repair, pre-monsoon water harvesting, and drought-tolerant variety promotion — are most effective when the drought pathway is confirmed as primary.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater depletion (over-extraction) causing water scarcity that mimics drought-induced shortage
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If seasonal_precipitation_mm and SPI are near-normal but farmers still report water scarcity, the pathway is more likely groundwater depletion than meteorological drought. Check SOGE classification for the block: if Semi-critical or above despite adequate rainfall, over-extraction is the primary driver. Drought pathway is confirmed when water scarcity co-occurs with documented below-normal SPI and VCI, and resolves in years with normal monsoon onset and rainfall.

confounders.confounder: Soil degradation (crusting, compaction, low infiltration on plateau slopes) reducing effective water availability independent of rainfall deficit
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Soil degradation reduces plant-available water even when rainfall is adequate, producing symptoms similar to drought stress. Distinguish by checking whether dry_spell_weeks and SPI are elevated in the same season as reported stress. If SPI is near-normal and dry spells are few but farmers report water stress, investigate soil hydraulic properties (crusting, low organic matter) as a confounding pathway. Drought pathway requires co-occurrence of meteorological deficit indicators.

recommended_solutions: Check dam repair using community labour ahead of monsoon
Emergency pre-monsoon water harvesting structure construction

sources.title: Drought assessment for kharif rice using standardized precipitation index (SPI) and vegetation condition index (VCI)
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that SPI and VCI during July–October are significantly correlated with kharif rice productivity index in UP districts (Allahabad, Kanpur, Lucknow), explaining 69–76% of yield variability; supports use of combined SPI+VCI threshold in sig_02 and drought_weeks signals in sig_01.

sources.title: Management of crop water under drought: a review
sources.relevance_note: Reviews site-specific drought stress hydrology and crop water management; supports dry spell duration thresholds (sig_03), monsoon onset delay effects (sig_05), and the rationale for drought-tolerant variety promotion as an intervention.

sources.title: Water–food–energy nexus with changing agricultural scenarios in India during recent decades
sources.relevance_note: Documents that after drought years, groundwater storage in India drops and fails to recover fully even after good monsoon years; supports seasonal and annual precipitation deficit thresholds in sig_04 and the confounder distinguishing drought from groundwater depletion pathways.

sources.title: Manual for Drought Management
authors: Government of India, Department of Agriculture, Cooperation and Farmers Welfare
sources.relevance_note: Defines operational drought classification using SPI, MAI, and VCI; provides the framework for drought_causality_json variable interpretation and the combined meteorological-agricultural drought confirmation logic used in sig_02.
